sentences.text: Sure.
I do not recall whether it took two days, two nights, three nights.
I honestly cannot remember.
But of course, there was no place to sit down or to lie down.
I mean, it was just really cramped in there.
And I-- I-- there could have been a bucket for-- for waste, but-- but unfortunately, if it was, it was filled up immediately.
So there was waste all over, human waste all over.
We did not get any water or any food to eat on that ride, so of course, people died in there.
When we arrived, I mean, they just take-- I guess maybe those people were the lucky ones.
It was impossible to escape.
I mean, the boxcars were just locked up and we had no tools or we didn't bring any the luggage with us even.
So of course, we-- we had nothing to escape there, so many people had died.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were all starved.
It was winter, so of course the first thing we were looking for a little bit of snow.
We also had immediate contact not only with the soldiers, and the soldiers were all just mad dogs.
Those were-- those were the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were constantly screaming and yelling.
And although we did understand German, but-- but the words they used, of course, were strange to me anyway and I didn't know them.
I couldn't understand the words they were saying.
And then the-- the-- there were prisoners there, too, which were already in the camps, a special breed of prisoners because they knew the faith, what is going to happen to most of the people.
And they just said, at least to-- when we were separated-- don't worry, you'll be OK.
You're coming into a camp, you're coming into a camp.
It's a working camp, don't worry.
We didn't dare even ask, what's happening there?
Were we going to see our parents, our sisters, our brothers?
None.
They wouldn't even answer us to that question.
But the Germans were brutal from the beginning, the SS.
I shouldn't say Germans, of course they were German.
As far as I knew, they were German.
They were brutal right from the start, constantly beating us-- constantly, constantly.
And of course, when we arrived into Birkenau, the following day we were all tattooed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were supposedly lining up in alphabetical order.
And my brother went, of course, before me.
I was the second one.
And his number was 98,909.
My tattoo on my arm reads 98,910, which means actual reality I was the 98,910th person arriving into the workforce, into the camps.
I'm quite sure there were many, many, many, many more which came in but they never made it even to the number.
So they only picked the strongest ones that they thought they could still work, they could still get some-- they still could be some use to the Third Reich.
So anyway, this is how I was taken into the camps.
The first night, they gave us the clothes.
And of course, we had the bunks, three tiers.
And-- and for example, I was a young one so I could climb up on the highest one, on the tallest one, on the third bunk.
And we had to leave our shoes in front of the bunks.
And of course exhausted, hungry, and no food.
And the following probably was 4 o'clock in the morning, they come in yelling-- you can't even understand them-- raus, raus, get up.
And how can you get your shoes?
Everybody's running for their shoes so you pick anything that you could-- that you could wear.
And-- and-- and they-- I remember this was, God February-- February.
Could have been February 1, February 2.
I have-- I have chosen the day of observance-- in our language it's called yahrzeit-- for my parents.
I believe we have arrived at February 2 or so into-- or January 31.
I honestly do not recall the exact date.
But I have chosen February 2 as the memorial day for my parents.
I figured they probably couldn't kill everybody the same night.
I learned otherwise later on.
But anyway, so I have chosen February 2 as the observance day for my yahrzeit, which corresponds to the Jewish calendar 27 days in Shabbat.
And I had to call here-- when I came to this country finally, I called a rabbi in here who established the date for me to correspond to February 2, what the Jewish date would be.
And this is my observance in there.
We spent-- I spent probably about five to six weeks in Birkenau.
They called it actually the quarantine.
Whoever died-- many people died in Birkenau, many of them.

As a matter of fact, the-- the people that I would say the wealthier, who had it good and had maids in the house and didn't do any physical work during their lifetime, they were the first ones to die.
My brother-in-law's brother died in my arms in Birkenau.
I mean, we weren't working even yet.
Of course, they didn't give us much food, either.
But many of these people, of our people, died days after they have arrived.

And all-- when-- when you died, all you had to do is throw the guys out-- out of the barracks in a-- a little buggy, two-wheeled buggy would come along with-- with prisoners pushing it and collect all the corpses and take them off.
Because they were burning them, taking them to the crematoriums.
And again, I must tell you, those people were probably the lucky ones.
Because there were many times-- oh, yes, even the first night of arrival in there-- a lot of the people, maybe they knew.
I don't know why, but they started running and they ran towards the barbed wires.
And of course, there were-- those were electric, electrified wires.
And of course, they were just-- all you could hear is the screaming and they were electrocuted right there.
And later on after being in the camps, I wondered whether those were the lucky ones that didn't have to go through what we went through, that they died instantly.
We couldn't even think of our parents, sisters, brothers, cousins.
You have become the number one to survive.
You were worried strictly about yourself.
We learned very fast what happened to the other people, what happened to our loved ones.
You could smell the stench of the-- of the burning of the flesh which was going on day and night, day and night constantly.
And we knew exactly when transports would arrive.
And then especially being in Birkenau, I was there at the same time the gypsies were there, [INAUDIBLE].
And they have liquidated-- one morning, we woke up and there was not a trace of the-- we were right across from each other.
The gypsy camp was liquidated completely and there were many, many young-- young people, young persons.
I guess they were called also the-- probably lower than the Jews.
They were just completely undesirable.
They were called parasites.
We were called parasites anyway all the time, but they were probably just even a step lower than us and they liquidated that camp.
After spending I believe it was about five weeks, I believe it was-- could have been five weeks, less time-- time was really unimportant, it just went.
By the way, the barracks were just very long barracks.
And they had like a brick chimney-- chimney all the way through the barracks.
Supposedly, you put some wood and-- to get some heat.
There was never wood to burning anyway to heat the place.
And supposedly, all the bricks would get warm.
This is familiar to me because of the brick factory, which we had.
But there was never any heat in there.
And-- and the food that we had it was just-- they we just bring a little bit of water, supposedly warm.
And there were some-- I don't know, leaves, vegetables in there.
But we would get a small piece of bread.
And the first-- no, I'm jumping a little bit ahead.
Then after about the four or five weeks, we were marched in to Auschwitz, which is an adjoining camp.
Birkenau was very close to Auschwitz.
We marched into Auschwitz and we get-- and we had different barracks again.
I do not remember my barrack number that I was in, but those were brick buildings.
I guess they must have been either built for that purpose or they were built for-- for soldiers-- for soldiers barracks to be in there.
And I remember the first night when they gave us a piece of bread and I went to sleep and I put the bread in my striped uniform pants, rolled the pants up, put it under my-- under my head.
Of course, when I woke up in the morning, it was gone.
The bread wasn't there.
That was my first lesson.
And since then-- since then, every time I got food, they can't take it out of my stomach.
I immediately ate everything which I ever had laid my hands on.
As a matter of fact, even the-- the-- the-- they called them stubendienst, which means the guy in charge of the barrack, which was an inmate also, but he was above everybody else.
He had some special privileges.
He was dishing out the food and he had better food to eat than us.
They were very strict.
If they ever caught a guy that would steal your piece of bread, he actually was sentenced to death.
They would kill him.
Inmates would kill him simply because we all received the same piece of bread.
If you take it away from me, that means I'm going to starve to death.
And this was-- it's like a code of honor between-- between the-- between the prisoners, between us.
But anyway, of course I didn't know who took it.
And from then on, as I indicated, whatever I got, I ate immediately.
They took me to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Bauhof commander.
They were usually asking for certain tradespeople.
This was really a-- a-- a-- a joke with them because everybody felt, well, if I'm a tradesman, I have a better chance to survive.
They're going to need me so I can become anything-- whatever they wanted me to become.
And they would say they need bricklayers.
Fine, bricklayers?
I'm a bricklayer.
How hard is it to lay bricks, especially I-- we used to manufacture bricks.
OK.
So they took you to unload cement trucks.
I figured, you-- you want to build?
Well, you got to unload the cement trucks.
And it was a very hard job because we weren't strong.
We-- we-- we were weak people in there.
And so for-- for about the next, I'd say three, four months, I worked at the-- at the Bauhof commander, which means unloading cars of cement.
During that time, my brother took ill.
He had a terrible infection in one of the-- his toes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, the Germans loved experimentation.
Of course, they took him into the hospital and I guess he made it and they-- but I didn't know where he was.
I-- I-- I didn't know.
At night-- not at night, there was a curfew, but in wintertime especially or early spring-- [DRINKS] --I remember taking a candle so maybe he could look down.
Because a lot of us prisoners, I mean, we had someone in the hospital.
So we-- so to find out if they're still alive or not.
So we took a candle-- we got some candles, don't ask me where I got a candle-- but anyhow-- and I went and lit the candle so he can see me.
I couldn't see him at night.
He recognized me and finally, I found him, he was.
And what they have done is they experimented really on him.
They have cut his-- they split his toe, actually, into the big toe, one of his legs.
And they applied certain things and actually, he made it.
And he made it and he came back to us.
About a month after this, we were-- they were choosing some people again.
This time they needed plumbers.
Of course, I become a plumber suddenly.
And they-- they were evacuating us to another camp.
And they only needed so many and so many.
And I was the last one they took.
And this would have been the first separation from my brother.
But as it happened-- for example, they needed, let's say, 400 people.
One of the people which was taken to go to that camp suddenly died.
So they needed another one.
My brother was next in line [INAUDIBLE].
So this is how we wound up together.
And I do not recall now whether we-- they marched us or we went on trucks to the new camp, which was called Buna or they called it Monowitz.
This was operated by the IG Farben industries in Germany.
The camp itself was just being built.
We had to build it, as some of the barracks weren't completed yet.
And we had to bring in stones, every day go in the fields and bring in stones so they can make-- so they can make a little road for them to come in there.
And we were working in the-- for the IG Farben industries.
We walked out of the camp every single day in lines of whether it was five or 10.
And can you imagine an orchestra playing, us going out to work and an orchestra playing so we can march so they could have it-- they could count easier.
I mean, left, right, left, right and they would just count the lines-- you know, five a line, five in a line, five in a line.

And for example, the kapo which was the-- the foreman of that group would announce-- usually they were Germans, also inmates, political prisoners or otherwise or thieves or whatever, bandits-- and they would have that job.
And they would report.
They would be the first ones coming out to work and they would say, for example, my kommando was 70.
I was a painter.
I guess I couldn't make a plumber or they had enough plumbers, so I suddenly become a painter.
And they-- he would-- he would be in front of the lines and he would say, kommando 70 with 200 people going out to work.
And that-- that total, of course, you had to come back also.
And they would count just the lines and mingle.
And-- and this is how we would march out to work and go into the factory.
As a painter, we would-- first, we would have those metal brushes and take the rust off steel sheets.
I don't know what they were, but they were steel sheets.
Then we would paint them.
And then they'd build those huge gasometers.
I don't know what was inside-- could have been oil, could have been gas.
But this is what we were doing in building it.
First-- first taking off the rust and then they would build it and then we would paint it again and again and again.
I always volunteered to work high up.
Why?
Because the Germans themselves were afraid to climb high.
I was young and I could work at my own pace.
In other words, I know work had to be done, but I would work at my own pace once I was up there.
I wasn't being hit by them and I could also, pardon me the expression, but take my shirts off and pick the lice out there, is summertime time especially.
In wintertime we used to work on the inside.
So I would always work there.
Unfortunately, my brother was separated from me in that camp.
In every kommando, we were painters so we occupied a certain barrack.
For example, our barrack was number six.
They knew that barrack six are painting-- is a painter kommando.
Now, what happened when somebody died?
So they were always requisite.
They would, again, try and find out if they needed a painter, who is a painter.
You don't have to be-- don't have to have a college diploma or be a rocket scientist to become a painter.
You take a-- a brush and you paint.
And that's exactly what I was doing.
And this is why, by the way, I always hated painting after the war and even up to date.
I still hate painting.
I don't like doing the paintwork.
Anyway, so my brother was working-- they called it the cable kommando.
Cable kommando means that you had to carry the cables on your back and pull it.
I mean, there would be 10, 15 guys, 20 guys pulling it.
And of course, my brother was tall and of course, the tall guy would always have the problem, he was the tallest he would have the biggest burden.

And-- and he-- I knew-- I recognized that my brother would never make it because usually people who worked at those cable kommandos, they would only last a certain period because of the malnutrition and being constantly in mud and in rain.
It wouldn't work.
We could not do the work even when it rained.
I mean, we could not do the work outside and do the painting.
So we were always taken inside.
They always had enough pipes.
And I don't know what in the hell they-- they had going through the pipes.
It must have been some gas or so.
I know that the pipes were also refrigerated, which means they were always cold and you could see ice forming on them.
So I had no idea what they were in there-- doing there.
So I went to my kapo.

His name was Kapo Louis, I don't remember his last name-- although I looked for him after the war because he was-- he was really-- although he was German and he was a prisoner, but he was a political prisoner.
And the political prisoners are like-- I believe he must have been a Communist or so, that's why he was in concentration camp.
But he was a very fair man.
So I-- knowing that my brother will-- eventually he'll-- he'll just give up, I went to the kapo and I told him this is the only relative I have in my life.
And we were-- we were three young boys, the youngest one of the group in there.
One is still in Chicago today, Morris, he's around here, and the other one is in Israel.
His name is Joshua.
And we were working together and we were always getting the easiest jobs because he would assign the jobs what to do.
And they would always give us an extra quart of water or an extra quart of soup because we were the youngest ones.
So I went to him one day, I figured I had nothing to lose, and told him, look, I said, my brother is working the cable kommando so he knew right away what I wanted.
And I said, he's going to be gone very soon.
And I said, he can hardly walk already.
I said, can you get him into our kommando?
And he did it for me.
He-- he wanted-- he said he needs another painter in there and he knows of a painter in another group and they transferred him to our-- to our kommando.
Now, when he came into our kommando, we weren't getting any extra foods or it wasn't an elite group.
But our work was much easier, not unloading trains with cement and not working with coal.
We were painters.
And even if you have to paint, you can paint slow.
So there's a little story which goes with it.
So my brother came into work and there were also small we called them gasometers, small tanks maybe about-- well, five feet in diameter while the other one could have been 40 and 50 feet in diameter.
Those were small ones.
We had to paint these, too.
I don't know why.
Never asked why.
Anyway, they wouldn't tell us.
So when my brother came to work there, he was doing work for 10 people.
I mean, we always were questioning him, are you setting a bad precedent?
But in comparison to what he was doing before, you know, what he's doing now- I mean, it was a plaything for him.
So the German-- we had a German foreman still wearing his swastika on his arm.
And he didn't like me because he knew I wasn't doing the work and yet he was afraid to go and check.
I was working about 60, 70 feet on top of a gasometer.
So my brother, he recognized a man that is working.
And my brother would work so hard because to him it was not work.
It was just sitting on his fanny and doing some scrubbing and some painting.
But this Nazi would bring him food-- would bring him bread, would bring him salami.
He says, you're the only man that works in here.
He says, but, you sit and eat it here.
You can't give any to your brother, he's a lazy bum.
That was me.
Anyway.
And-- and-- and he would have enough food.
I had my food.
How did I get extra food in the camps?
At one time, we also worked with civilian people.
Not forced labor, but they were working also for the IG Farben industries as painters, as professional because we were not professionals.
And they would wear civilian clothes.
They would come in.
And this particular man, his name was Kolojejchick.

He was a Polish man who lived about-- it was Bielsko, which must have been about-- I don't know how many miles from the camp which we were, could have been 20 miles or so away from the camp or maybe 30 miles away from the camp.
He would come in, he would stay someplace near the camps at a place.
And we got acquainted because we worked together on the job.
And as I said, I was a youngster or looked young at the time.
And he kind of took a liking to me.
And I noticed that he is buying shirts from some people.
Well, I had no shirts to sell.
Well, don't ask me how, but I got one shirt.
Whether I stole it or whether they gave an extra shirt, I never gave back the old ones, I was wearing the old one and kept the new one.
And I told him, I have a shirt, I want to sell it.
And the shirt was torn.
So he gave me a loaf of bread for the shirt.
Fine.
The following day, I told him, look-- this is the way I've been all my life, I guess.
I said, my conscience is bothering me.
The shirt was torn.
I said, I'm sorry but I needed the bread.
He said, I know the shirt was torn.
He says, don't you worry about it.
From then on, this man would bring me his rations every single week.
They would get, for example, some salami, bread, and margarine.
He would bring it into the factory.
I couldn't bring it into camp.
He would bring it into the factory at a certain spot that he would leave it for me.
And I had more food than the rest of the guys.

I had like it's-- this is how I brought my brother back, actually, to life because that Nazi only gave him food a couple of times or so or when he didn't finish his lunch, he probably would give him the leftovers.
But we had food.
And as a matter of fact, I tried to look for this guy after the war.
I wrote away to the city describing the man and giving his name, but I never had a reply.
During the first-- so anyway, we had food.
And I remember one time in the camps, he for Christmas vacation, I guess, Christmastime, he took off.
And he left food with a guy which used to work a coal mountain.
In other words, they were putting coals in one particular area.
This is the coal they were using at the factories.
And there was a guard usually guarding it-- guarding it-- I don't know why-- who would steal it.
But anyway, and he would leave it with this guy.
This guy knew that he was giving me bread.
So I didn't even know about it, but we worked so close to him that the guy came to me.
He says, you know, he's not here but he brought in some food for you.
And so I figured, I've got to do something for the man once I came to the United States.
But anyway, I couldn't.
During the first bombardment of that camp that I was there, they actually-- the Americans bombed the factory.
Some of the bombs fell in our camp also.
But when the first-- they bombed the place for the first time, this man came to me, Kolojejchick, and he says to me, here is my clothes.
Take my-- take my good clothes, because he was wearing the working clothes as a painter.
He says, follow me.
He says, I can hide you.
And this was in about August, September of 1944.
And when they bombed the-- the factory, all the Nazis, all the SS run away, too.
They-- a lot of the prisoners ran out of the factory because they didn't-- besides having guards with us at work, they also had surrounded the entire factory, which was fenced in, of course.
And there were guards outside the factory.
But when they started bombing, everybody ran and everybody ran out of the factory.
And I told him, I said, Mr.
Kolojejchick, I cannot run without my brother.
He says, I cannot hide two people.
I can hide one, but I can't hide two or it will be too suspicious and impossible.
I said, I thank you but I cannot run without him.
And I didn't, of course.
I-- I-- I didn't know what the end will be.
But the-- the desire to survive, the desire to avenge or revenge were so great that-- that-- that-- that-- that you do think.
You you, you-- you wanted to survive and number two is that you were always the number one in the camp.
You didn't care what happens to the other.
I mean, you-- you-- once you allowed this to happen to you, of course, you were a goner, too.
Now, I don't know how we-- not me, but the Jewish people, the more learned ones, the rabbis, they knew when there was a Jewish holiday in there.
Because even in the camps which didn't have anything to eat, but when it came the holy days, like Yom Kippur now, we were fasting.
We weren't even eating then.
So somehow, we knew.
Without a calendar, I don't know how the Jewish people knew.
But maybe they were marking off every day and we knew.
Anyway, of course, once we were inside the barracks, we would-- we could pray if we wanted to, but only if you knew it by heart.
I mean, there was nothing to pray from.
Now, the barracks, again, there were three tiers to sleep in there.
And at night you had a-- a-- a-- there was bucket in there.
And-- and of course, if you have to go, you have to go.
And every time you had to go, of course, it-- when the bucket was filled up.
It-- it-- it's a little humor in here.
When the bucket was filled up, of course the last guy doing it had to carry it out and they would take down his number to make sure that he comes back.
So nobody-- everybody was afraid.
So everybody was just waiting for the guy to take it out and then suddenly, bingo, in the next five minutes, the bucket is full again and somebody has to go and pick it up.
So we were all having fun with it at night.
I mean, sitting around or waiting in your-- in your bunk bed until somebody took it out.

Every time you left the barracks, if you had to go really-- if you have to go to the toilet for a bowel movement or so, they would, of course, write down your number you could go-- undressed, of course, going in there.
Now, we will get a-- a-- This is the end of tape number two.
